coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot arrays: Pros and cons of photodetection in the Coulomb blockade regime
coredata.dc:creator: Abid,
Sehrawat, Poonam
Islam, S.S.
coredata.dc:description: Photon detection at elevated temperatures is a long-term debatable issue. Efforts are on to extend the temperature range beyond RT and this has been accomplished to a certain extent by combining two factors –(i) device’ structural design, and (ii) degree of quantum confinement. Zero-dimensional quantum confined semiconductor heterostructures have been investigated both theoretically and experimentally for quite some time; but no reports are available whether Quantum Dots is worthy for photodetection in Coulomb blockade regime and possess, if any, virtue to extend the operating temperature. In this report, Graphene Quantum Dots array (GQDs), were subjected to detect high photon density in the temperature range 77–623 K to check the sustainability of its noise free and quality performance. Stable performance is found up to 530 K beyond which the detector performance got badly affected owing to carrier transport and relaxation mechanisms. Appreciable detection performance is found in the Coulomb blockade (CB) regime (93–138 K) where carrier charging and tunneling through QDs control the detection mechanism. In non-CB regime (138–623 K), normal activation energy induced photodetection takes place. Detector performance shows that the user can opt for both the temperature regimes, though there are marked differences between them in terms of detection mechanism and performance.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of some physical parameters of QDs embedded in rGO maze - R(T) studies in the range 273–12 K
Charge carrier transport mechanism in Coulomb blockade regime
Carrier charging and tunneling studies under permutation of bias voltage, photon power density and operating temperature
IV plots as a function of photon power density at–(a) 300 K, (b) 138 K, and (c)103 K
Study of CB phenomena: circuit bias dependent photoconductive response at temperature 103 K, 138 K and 300 K for laser excitation wavelength 405 nm and 1064 nm
GQD arrays: theory of photocarrier generation and loss under photoexcitation, temperature and bias field
Wavelength dependent sensing response in GQD
Operating temperature dependent GQD sensor response when illuminated with laser wavelength 1064 nm
Photoresponsitivity and operating temperature of GQD based sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
XPS spectra of (a) GO (0 h), and (b-e) rGO samples: (b) 1 hr, (c) 2 hr, (d) 3 hr, and (e) 5 hr reduction time. (f) Changes in
s
p
2
carbons with reduction time. Inset to (f) shows structural evolution of rGO at different reduction steps, such that dark areas are
s
p
2
carbon patches, and light grey areas indicate
s
p
3
carbon which consists of oxygenated moieties (shown by small dots). Reproduced with permission from Ref. [56]. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Aberration-adjusted TEM micrographs of a single layer GO on a 2 nm scale. (a) Left side micrograph is 1 nm2 expanded oxidized portion in GO. Proposed atomic structure is shown in the centre where grey and red dots are carbon and oxygen atoms, respectively. The right-side image shows average simulated TEM image of the proposed structure and a simulated TEM micrograph of another structure having different positions of oxygenated moieties. (b) Zoomed image of the white spot in graphitic domain. The spot moved along the graphitic domain, but remained frozen for three frames (6s) at a hydroxyl position (left side of (b)) and for seven frames (14s) at a (1,2) epoxy position (right side of (b)). The suggested atomic arrangements of these functionalities are shown by ball-and-stick models below the microscope images. The simulated TEM image for the suggested structure agrees well with observed TEM structure. (c) a 1 nm2 graphitic portion from the exit plane wave reconstruction of a focal series of GO and the atomic structure of this region. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [57]. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) Raman spectra, (b) XRD patterns, and (c) UV–Vis spectra of GO and rGO (reduced for 5 hr). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photograph of the self-standing rGO/Alumina nanocomposite film [55]. - Published by The Royal Society of Chemistry. Illustrations of the (b) photodetection setup to record photoresponse, and (c) directions of applied electric field and irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Temperature dependent I-V characteristics of the GQD photodetector, and inset shows the variation of threshold voltage with temperature. (b) Resistance versus temperature plot in 273–12K temperature window. Inset depicts R (in log scale) verses 1000/T plot. The dotted, solid and dashed lines are fits to
R
(
T
)
=
R
0
e x p
(
T
0
/ T )
p
of experimental data of (b), using p=1, 1/3, and 1/2, respectively. [55] - Published by The Royal Society of Chemistry. (c) R versus T
−1/2
plot in semilog scale. Solid black line is the linear fit for T
−1/2
dependence of the experimental data (red circles). [55] - Published by The Royal Society of Chemistry. (d) I-V plots in –10mV to +10 mV voltage range showing the Coulomb staircase. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematics of a quantum dot coupled weakly to two leads (or terminals) via tunnel barriers. (b) The charge imbalance between QD and lead (maintained by CB) oscillates with external electrostatic potential difference (
φ
e x t
) as a sawtooth function. (c) Variation of conductance, G, with
φ
e x t
, indicating tunneling through degeneracy points of the charge imbalance. Thus, G demonstrates periodic oscillations in consonance with the charge imbalance. (d) Single-electron tunneling through a QD. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Laser Power dependent I-V characteristics of the developed sensor at 1064 nm illumination at (a) 103 K and (b) 138 K, and (c) 300 K. The inset to (a) shows the threshold voltage for CB current suppression as a function of power density at 103 K. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Laser Power dependent Resistance versus time plot indicating Coulomb oscillations for 405 nm laser at (a) 103 K and (b) 138 K. (c) Variation of peak-to-peak amplitude of oscillations in sensor response at 1064 nm illumination with temperature. Laser power dependent resistance versus time plot indicating Coulomb oscillations for 1064 nm laser at (d) 103 K and (e) 138 K. (f) Variation of peak-to-peak amplitude of oscillations in sensor response at 1064 nm illumination with temperature. (g) Resistance versus time plot showing fluctuations in resistance with fluctuations of temperature, i.e.
T = 35 K ± 0.3 K
. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Bias dependent Resistance versus time plot indicating Coulomb oscillations for 405 nm laser at (a) 103 K and (b) 138 K for 1.4 mW/mm2 power density. (c) Variation of peak-to-peak amplitude of oscillations in sensor response at 405 nm illumination with applied bias voltage. Bias dependent Resistance versus time plot indicating Coulomb oscillations for 1064 nm laser at (d) 103 K and (e) 138 K for 1.4 mW/mm2 power density. (f) Variation of peak-to-peak amplitude of oscillations in sensor response at 1064 nm illumination with applied bias voltage. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic diagram showing generation and loss of carriers during operation of QD as photodetector, and (b) example of single LO phonon relaxation, and multiple phonon relaxation from upper state to ground state within QD. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photocurrent as a function of time for ON/OFF condition of illumination with wavelengths (a) 405 nm, (b) 635 nm, and (c) 1064 nm. (d) Variation in photoresponsivity with illumination wavelength. All these studies are carried out at 1.4 mW/mm2 power density at a constant temperature of 300 K. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photocurrent versus time plot of the fabricated sensor at low temperature range 273–93 K. The experiment was conducted at laser wavelength 1064 nm having power density of 1.4 mW/mm2 for 12 V DC bias. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Variation of response and recovery time with operating temperature for 1064 nm laser excitation at a constant power density of 1.4 mW/mm2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Variation of (a) photocurrent, (b) photoresponsivity, (c) Sensitivity with operating temperature of the developed sensor. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot arrays: Pros and cons of photodetection in the Coulomb blockade regime

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Jamia Millia Islamia (A Central University), New Delhi, 110025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photon detection at elevated temperatures is a long-term debatable issue. Efforts are on to extend the temperature range beyond RT and this has been accomplished to a certain extent by combining two factors –(i) device’ structural design, and (ii) degree of quantum confinement. Zero-dimensional quantum confined semiconductor heterostructures have been investigated both theoretically and experimentally for quite some time; but no reports are available whether Quantum Dots is worthy for photodetection in Coulomb blockade regime and possess, if any, virtue to extend the operating temperature.

In this report, Graphene Quantum Dots array (GQDs), were subjected to detect high photon density in the temperature range 77–623 K to check the sustainability of its noise free and quality performance. Stable performance is found up to 530 K beyond which the detector performance got badly affected owing to carrier transport and relaxation mechanisms. Appreciable detection performance is found in the Coulomb blockade (CB) regime (93–138 K) where carrier charging and tunneling through QDs control the detection mechanism. In non-CB regime (138–623 K), normal activation energy induced photodetection takes place. Detector performance shows that the user can opt for both the temperature regimes, though there are marked differences between them in terms of detection mechanism and performance.

ce:section.ce:para: As on today, the photodetection is generally done either at 77 K or at extremely low temperatures, i.e. close to liquid helium temperature [1–3]. Room temperature operation is by far a distant dream and the limitation comes from the poor detection performance due to decrease in signal to noise ratio; leading to subsequent decrease in photoresponsivity and sensitivity. By and large, the material and the temperature of operation are the core issues and the rest are secondary. A combined strategy has been undertaken over the years to address the bottlenecks and few achievements have been made through semiconductor heterostructures [4–8].

Since the initial studies on quantum dots (QDs) and emergence of molecular beam epitaxy, low-dimensional semiconducting structures have gained immense interest [9–16]. Owing to quantum confinement, QDs, as zero-dimensional (0D) structures, possess unusual optoelectronic properties. They typically comprise one semiconducting material encapsulated by another material. Quantum dot photodetectors (QDPs) are the successor of quantum well photodetectors (QWPs), and only differ from QWPs in terms of the degree of confinement [9–12]. Detection principle in QDs involves inter-subband transitions between quantized energy levels of the dots and continuous states [10,17,18]. QDs also hold potential to increase detection sensitivity across wide spectrum including the visible range [13,19]. These considerations stimulate research in: (a) novel materials and structures to expand absorption spectrum, (b) minimize film thickness required for complete absorption and amplification of signal, (c) higher operating temperature, and (d) high efficiency and low cost. Another consideration in this direction includes integration of such materials with silicon electronics [20] and flexible substrates [21].

Our work is on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) wherein nanoscale phenomena such as quantum confinement play a crucial role [22]. Irrespective of their size, GQDs comprise graphitic structures inside the dot, endowing them with some of the extraordinary properties of graphene [22–24]. This renders them as model systems for investigating various quantum phenomena and backbone for future nanoelectronic devices on sensor applications. GQDs are developed as a byproduct during synthesis of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) from graphene oxide (GO). They gain from quantum size effects, such as variation in bandgap of the material with size of its nanocrystals. Owing to quantum confinement, charge carriers inside a QD occupy discrete energy levels just like individual atoms [24]. Carrier confinement also affects the charge transport mechanisms such as delocalization [25], variable-range hopping (VRH) [26], slow carrier relaxation [27], and electron–hole interactions [28–31]. Strong quantum confinement can help in enhancing detectivity, detection in visible spectrum [18,25], broadband tunability in one single material assembly, along with high temperature operation. However, graphene's zero bandgap at Dirac cones makes it almost transparent to any wavelength of irradiation, although it is considered to be good for an ideal photodetector. However, its zero bandgap leads to fast carrier recombination rates (∼ps) resulting in poor photo-responsivity, severely hampers any prospects of commercial application as photodetectors [32–34]. Bandgap tailoring [33–35] through strong quantum confinement effects presents an attractive solution to this concern. Nonetheless, the complex fabrication steps involved necessitate search for novel graphene like materials which can match its properties. This can be achieved via transformation of graphene oxide (GO) to reduced graphene oxide (rGO). GO has a bandgap ∼2.2 eV, whereas it varies from ∼1.00 eV to 1.69 eV in rGO, as a function of reduction efficiency [36–40].

During chemical transformation from GO to rGO, two distinct phenomena occur – (1) development of graphene patches (sp2 bond region) in the rGO sheet with an average size of ∼3–6 nm. These patches act like QDs and the intermediate space between dots are sp3 bonded region of insulating nature, as if the dots are caged within an insulating surface and, (2) creation of defect states outside QDs due to the presence of partial oxygen content remained after prolonged reduction process and 100% oxygen removal is never possible [41–45]. In this backdrop, it is interesting to see whether GQDs array will benefit the photodetection phenomena in terms of operating temperature and detection performance.

In this paper, we report the photodetection performance of GQDs array in terms of its operating temperature, photoresponsivity (Rλ), and sensitivity (S%). The sensors were operated over a wide temperature range (93–623 K) to check not only the sustainability of its noise free performance but also the qualitative gain in its detection parameters. The GQDs array shows Coulomb blockade (CB) phenomenon, and its side effects such as Coulomb blockade energy oscillation (CBO) and formation of envelope oscillation. Envelope shaped oscillations occur due to Coulomb force arising from interdot coupling under special conditions which have been explained in detail. Comprehensive charge carrier transport mechanisms are given for CB and non-CB regimes; and are linked to explain the practical photodetection performance of the GQDs array as a photodetector embedded in an rGO film.

Oxidation of graphite flakes using modified Hummers’ method resulted in formation of graphite oxide [34,46–48]. This graphite oxide was exfoliated in DI water under bath sonication to obtain graphene oxide (GO). In comparison to pristine graphite, GO being highly oxidized, contains numerous oxygenated functional moieties such as (a) carbonyl and carboxyl groups (sheet edges on
s
p
2
-carbon), and (b) hydroxyl and epoxy groups on
s
p
3
-carbon (basal plane) [49,50]. These oxygen containing groups cause easy dispersion of GO sheets in DI at low sonication powers [51]. Washing and subsequent drying of this suspension yielded GO powder which was then chemically reduced by adding 500 mg GO in 500 mL DI and sonicating the mixture for 1hr. Following this, 50 μL hydrazine hydrate was added into this mixture which was then refluxed at 98 °C [44,50] for different time periods of 1, 2, 3, and 5 hrs. These suspensions were then filtered and washed and lastly dried at 60 °C in inert atmosphere to obtain rGO powder.

Conversion of GO to rGO creates Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) in rGO maze with a size varying between few nm to 100 nm, based on the extent of
s
p
3
to
s
p
2
conversion as shown in Fig. 1 . The graphene region (
s
p
2
bonding) usually shows semiconducting behavior due to quantum confinement whereas the remaining
s
p
3
portions are insulator. Physical properties of rGO depend on
s
p
2
to
s
p
3
hybridized carbon ratio, and the type of functional group present. Thus, electrical conduction in rGO sheets is complex and demonstrates Coulomb blockade, specifically at low temperatures [50,52–55].

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze step by step changes in carbon and oxygen groups through gradual ejection of oxygenated moieties. The resulting XPS spectra showing GO to rGO transformation is included in Figure (a–e), indicating various bonds of carbon atoms. These include: (1) non-oxygenated CC bond at 284.5eV ± 0.4eV; (2) COH bond at 286eV ± 1eV; (3) CO bond at 287.3eV; and (4) C(O)O or OCOH bond at 289.2eV ± 0.4eV. Fig. 1(f) indicates that
s
p
2
carbon fraction (or reduction efficiency) increases from ∼57% for GO (0 h reduction) to nearly 80% for rGO reduced for 5 hr.

From Fig. 1(f) we can say that reduction process restores the
s
p
2
bonds. Nevertheless, a continuous
s
p
2
phase does not form; rather numerous small
s
p
2
‘grains’ appear spatially separated by disordered
s
p
3
domains. These observations have been made by Erickson et al. in Ref. [57], and are shown in Fig. 2 , revealing the confinement of graphitic
s
p
2
domains by
s
p
3
sea. The average size of
s
p
2
clusters varies between 2 and 6 nm.

The degree of graphitization can be understood at atomic scale by Raman spectroscopy. The most significant peaks in graphene and other graphitic materials include D-, G-, and 2D-bands at ∼1350 cm−1, ∼1580 cm−1, and ∼2700 cm−1, respectively and are well documented in literature [58,59]. The D-band arises from disorders in atomic structure, edge effects, ripples, or charge puddles of graphene film. A broad and highly intense D-band with respect to G-band signifies the presence of large amount of disorders in rGO. While the in-plane vibrations of
s
p
2
-carbon atoms result in G-band; 2D-band originates from second order Raman scattering and is dominant in graphene than bulk graphite [60]. The ratio of D- and G-band intensities is a quantitative measure of defects, and is depicted as
s
p
2
/
s
p
3
carbon ratio [59,61]. Fig. 3 (a) shows the Raman spectra of GO and rGO (reduced for 5hr). Using Tuinstra-Koenig empirical relation [58,62,63] between the ID/IG ratio and size of graphitic
s
p
2
zone, the size of ordered graphitic regions was found to be ∼3.3 nm [58].

The degree of conversion of GO to rGO structure was investigated via X-ray diffractometer and the patterns are shown in Fig. 3(b). The peak at 10°, corresponding to GO, disappears after reduction and a new peak corresponding to rGO appears at 25.6° [48]. Fig. 3(c) shows UV–Vis spectra taken for further confirmation of GO to RGO transformation. The absorbance spectra reveal the prominent peak signifying absorption in UV band, representing
π –
π
∗
transitions of aromatic CC bonds [34,64]. Besides, the peak at 239 nm (related to GO) red shifts to 267 nm indicating the reduction of GO.

The post synthesis rGO (5 hr reduction) as described in Section 2.1 was used to fabricate the photodetector. Self-supporting and flexible film of rGO/Alumina composite was prepared by following the sol-gel route wherein we mixed 0.6 wt% rGO powder in 99.4 wt% alumina sol as explained in Refs. [65,66]. Fig. 4 (a) displays the developed self-standing rGO/Alumina composite film. To fabricate photodetector, we cut slices of 10 mm × 10 mm dimension from this film and deposited silver electrodes at their end terminals. The electrodes were dried in ambient for 24 hr. The optical sensing measurements were performed in a two-probe system fitted with liquid nitrogen closed cycle system (Fig. 4(b)). The radiation exposure driven photoresponse was examined in 93–623 K temperature window and was recorded via Keithley 4200 SCS system. The directions of applied electric field (to sweep photogenerated carriers across the end terminals of sensor) and irradiation are depicted in Fig. 4(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of some physical parameters of QDs embedded in rGO maze - R(T) studies in the range 273–12 K
ce:section.ce:para:
Current-voltage (IV) measurements were conducted in 273–12 K temperature range by placing the fabricated photodetector in a four probe Helium closed cycle cryostat (Oxford Instruments) to get fundamental information about: (i) Coulomb blockade as well as non-blockade regime and their temperature dependent electrical behavior; (ii) localization length of the wave function inside the QD along with the average size of GQDs; and (iii) the charge carrier transport mechanism in CB as well as non-CB regime. The IV plots shown in Fig. 5 (a) appear highly symmetric and give evidence of current suppression (
I = 0
) below a threshold voltage (
V
T H
). Due to symmetric nature of IV plots, Schottky barrier assisted current suppression can be ruled out in this case [62,67]; and we attribute this effect to Coulomb blockade phenomenon [67–71], where a conduction gap appears when the overall charging energy (
E
C
) of a QD satisfies
E
C
≫
K
B
T
. We have obtained, EC ∼30.2 meV [62], thus a Coulomb gap is expected only at
T ≪ 349
K. These findings are in agreement with the results obtained in Ref. [62] for rGO QDs based transistor with a gate bias voltage of 0 V. As evident from Fig. 5(a),
V
T H
linearly decreases with temperature till 94 K and becomes negative thereafter [68]. A negative threshold voltage has been ascribed to Coulomb blockade phenomenon by many researchers [68,72–75]. IV results clearly indicate the existence of two distinct regimes, i.e. Coulomb blockade and non-blockade; and it is expected that the charge-transport mechanism will also be regime specific.

It is well known that for unequal tunnel barriers (similar to our case), a stepwise increase of the current with increase in bias voltage is expected [76–79]. Thus, a current step ( Δ I ≈ e G / C ;
G
is conductance and
C
is the equivalent capacitance) occurs when the energy interval
e V
between the source and drain terminals contains an extra charge state [76]. The existence of energy level quantization within QD may be verified through the formation of Coulomb staircase as it was reported in case of SET in the IV plots shown in Refs. [76–79]. This Coulomb staircase is also observed in our two-terminal device as shown in Fig. 5(d). The width of the current plateau,
Δ V = 0.5
mV, represents the charging energy:
e Δ V =
e
2
/ C
. In order to see the effect of frequency on the Coulomb staircase, an ac signal having frequency 1 MHz having
± 276
mV (peak-to-peak) amplitude was applied to the sample and the results are plotted in Fig. 5(d). As seen from the figure, some of the plateaus (two neighboring plateaus) are missing or weakly developed, as expected from studies reported in Refs. [76,80]. In case of a SET, current steps changes from
Δ I = e
(without frequency) to
Δ I = e f
, for a signal with frequency, f. Since, our device comprises multiple dots with wide size distribution and unequal tunnel barriers, the change in
Δ I
is found
I = 8.93
nA at 1 MHz which is much higher than a SET.

The resistance versus temperature plot shown in Fig. 5(b) reveals enormous jump in resistance of nearly six orders of magnitude while decreasing temperature from 273 K to 12 K. Such huge variation in conductivity vis-à-vis graphene is attributable to the residual functional moieties (
s
p
3
carbons) and disorders present in rGO [58,68–71,81,82]. The semi-logarithmic plot shown in the inset of Fig. 5(b) reveals several distinct regions (also evident in TEM image), resembling disordered semiconductors [68,82].

XPS and Raman analyses indicate that an rGO sheet can be treated as a two-dimensional array of GQDs such that graphitic regions act like quantum dots and disordered domains behave as tunneling barriers [55]. Temperature dependent charge-transport mechanism in rGO can be explained by three independent phenomena, i.e. Mott variable range hopping (Mott-VRH) [58,83], Efros-Shklovski (ES-VRH) [50,81,84], and thermal activation [48,85] supported VRH or the Arrhenius behavior. Transport mechanism in GQDs array requires thorough understanding for graphene-based optoelectronics [86,87].

Resistance versus temperature response as shown in Fig. 5(b) can be attributed to the characteristic of strong localization region of variable range hopping (VRH). Mott [58,83] VRH considers the following cases –
a.
Creation of localized defect states near the Fermi level during GO to rGO transformation. In this case, carrier transport takes place through hopping between states by absorbing energy from either a phonon or an external electric field.
b.
At lower temperatures, nearest neighbour hopping is not important and hopping electrons strive for minimum activation energy (
Δ E
) and smallest hopping distance. Usually, both these situations do not arrive simultaneously and there exists a maximum hopping probability corresponding to an optimum hopping distance,
r
h o p
.
c.
No Coulomb interaction occurs between these localized states.

Thus, the standard Mott-VRH relation can be expressed as [58,83]:
(1)
R
(
T
)
=
R
0
exp
(
T
0
/ T )
p
where,
R
0
denotes zero temperature resistance and
T
0
is Mott characteristic temperature. The scaling factor
p
depends on the dimension of the system or the shape of density of states (DOS) [82,88]. This relation leads to a mathematical expression where
R
scales as
( T )
− 1 / 3
.

According to Efros-Shklovskii (ES), the localized defect states occur near the Fermi level. Charge transport takes place via hopping mechanism and Coulomb interaction is present among these localized states [81,84]. In ES-VRH,
R
varies as
( T )
− 1 / 2
[81–84], i.e.:
(2)
R
(
T
)
=
R
0
exp
(
T
0
/ T )
1 / 2

In Arrhenius theory of activation energy supported hopping,
p
is
− 1
and the equation becomes:
(3)
R
(
T
)
=
R
0
exp
(
E
a
/
k
B
T )

Linear fits have been obtained for Mott-VRH (p = 1/3), ES-VRH (p = 1/2) and activation energy (p = 1) assisted conduction mechanisms in the temperature ranges of 146–90 K, 90–12 K, and 273–146 K, respectively. Besides, the temperature range 77–50 K may be considered as an intermediate region where both ES- and Mott-VRH are coexisting. ES-VRH considers strong localization of wave functions in GQDs and it can be used to determine the localization length
ξ
by plotting R versus
T
− 1 / 2
in semilog scale (inset of Fig. 5(b)) [89–91]. From the slope of this plot,
T
0
= 4170
K is obtained.
T
0
is related to
ξ
through [62,81]:
(4)
T
0
≡
T
E S
=
(
2.8
e
2
4 π ε
ε
0
k
B
ξ
)
Where
ε
0
and
ε
are the values of permittivity of vacuum and the dielectric constant of the material.
ε
in rGO is 3.5. Using Equation (4), we have obtained
ξ
∼3.2 nm which agrees well with the GQD size determined from Raman analysis, representing strong localization of wave function inside the graphitic domain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge carrier transport mechanism in Coulomb blockade regime
ce:section.ce:para:
Coulomb blockade (CB) usually occurs in structures having a well-defined metallic grain (island) or quantum dot (QD), which is separated from the terminals by tunneling barriers. CB occurs where tunneling through QD with small capacitance is obstructed at low temperatures as well as low applied bias voltages. When the QD is initially neutral, it does not generate any significant electric field beyond its boundary. In this state, a weak external force (due to electric field) is required to bring in an additional electron from outside. This follows from the fact that addition of a single electron to such systems requires an electrostatic charging energy (
E
C
) of the order of
e
2
/
C
Σ
= π
r
2
≫
k
B
T
eV, where
C
Σ
is its effective capacitance, i.e.
1
C
Σ
=
1
C
T S
+
1
C
T D
,
T
the temperature and
V
the applied bias. This justification was provided by Gorter [92] for anomalous jump in the resistance of thin granular metallic films [93].

A single electron transistor (SET) is an appropriate model system to study Coulomb blockade phenomenon that includes a confined region (QD) weakly coupled to the two leads via tunneling barriers, with a gate electrode (not shown in Fig. 6 (a)) which controls the charge on QD. This charge has two components as shown in Fig. 6(a) [94,95]. A total of
N
conduction electrons on the QD contribute a charge
Q
=
− N e
, and this
Q
can only change in discrete amounts, i.e. ‘
e
‘. Nearby external charges (such as charges on the gate) induce a displacement charge (
C
φ
e x t
) on QD, and this charge can vary continuously. Here,
C
Σ
is the QD effective capacitance, and
φ
e x t
represents the electrostatic potential difference between QD and leads because of external charges. Thus, there exists a charge imbalance on the QD which is given by:
C
φ
e x t
− N e
(where
N
is an integer), and minimizing its absolute value can determine
N
at low temperatures. When this charge imbalance becomes
+
1
2
e
, a transition from
N
to
N + 1
electrons occurs on the QD. The additional, or the (N+1)th, electron results in reversing the sign of the charge imbalance to become
−
1
2
e
; therefore, the charge imbalance oscillates in a sawtooth fashion (Fig. 6(b)). At low temperatures, tunneling only occurs close to the points where the charge imbalance switches between
+
1
2
e
and
−
1
2
e
. In this case, the electrons tunnel through the QD, one at a time. The CB is cancelled out and (zero bias) conductance exhibits a peak (Fig. 6(c)). The conductance peak can be described using the following relation [93]:
(5)
E
N
∗
≡
E
N
+
(
N −
1
2
)
e
2
C
Σ
=
E
F
+ e
φ
e x t
E
N
∗
represents renormalized level spacing given by:
E
N
∗
= Δ E +
e
2
/
C
Σ
, and corresponds to an enhanced spacing above the bare level spacing. When
e
2
/
C
Σ
≪ Δ E
, Eq. (5) becomes the condition of resonant tunneling, whereas for
e
2
/
C
Σ
≫ Δ E
, Eq. (5) represents the periodicity of classical CBO in conductance versus gate voltage [93]. Fig. 6(d) is the schematic illustration of an electron tunneling through the dot, governed by Eq. (5). These are the “Coulomb-blockade oscillations” [96–100]. Apart from some early studies [101–103], the transport properties of GQDs need both theoretical and experimental illustration. Photodetection is one of many applications combining extraordinary properties of individual QDs and combined properties of coupled QDs [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carrier charging and tunneling studies under permutation of bias voltage, photon power density and operating temperature
ce:section.ce:para: In QD, charging and tunneling process depends on the availability of suitable external energy. If it is done by heat energy alone, then the onset of CBO and its envelope form oscillation solely depend on the temperature. In case of photodetection, apart from temperature, there are two other driving sources, i.e. applied DC bias and laser excitation energy, both will influence the onset as well as the domain of CB regime. In another way to say that the onset of CBO and its envelop formation will depend on the combinational effect of temperature, DC bias, and laser excitation energy altogether; this is demonstrated in the subsequent sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IV plots as a function of photon power density at–(a) 300 K, (b) 138 K, and (c)103 K
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 (a–c) shows IV characteristics as a function of photon power density and measurements were taken at different temperatures, i.e. 103 K, 138 K and 300 K, respectively. From Fig. 7, current flowing through the detector changes from few hundred nanometers at 103 K to few hundred microamperes at 300 K; thus, indicating a change of the order of 1000 when going from 103 K to 300 K. The region from 103 K to 138 K temperature range represents CB region and current flow is very low. Fig. 7(a) indicates the presence of CB region where power dependent threshold voltage has been calculated, found to decrease with increase in the laser power density (inset to Fig. 7(a)).

A combination of power density, excitation wavelengths, and temperature is made to check the influence on the onset of Coulomb blockade energy oscillations (CBO), envelope formation of CBO and how the energy oscillations die out, as is shown in Fig. 8 . It is observed that CBO ceased to exist at a power level specific to laser excitation wavelength and temperature. Even peak to peak amplitude (PTPA) of CBO decreases with increasing power density for both the laser excitations at both the temperature; and there is a large difference in PTPA of the oscillations at 103 K and 138 K (Fig. 8(c and f)). The observations indicate that CBO and its wave shape oscillations are tunable.

In order to prove that the envelope formation is not due to noise, we created artificial temperature instability by intentionally causing some leakage in the cryostat chamber. As a result, the chamber pressure went down from ∼10−7 torr to ∼10−4 torr, where temperature fluctuates by
Δ T = ± 0.3
K. Because of this, we have observed the resistance fluctuations as a function of time where there is no indication of any envelope formation (Fig. 8(g)). This measurement was carried out from 77 K to 12.76 K and no envelope formation was observed, thereby supporting our claim that it is not a noise, rather CB energy oscillations, and envelope formation due to interdot coupling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of CB phenomena: circuit bias dependent photoconductive response at temperature 103 K, 138 K and 300 K for laser excitation wavelength 405 nm and 1064 nm
ce:section.ce:para: The DC electrical bias across the sensor plays a vital role on the transportation of the carriers that tunnel through QD and slip in the barrier region (insulator). More the field in the channel, more are the carriers collected across the terminal, and hence more is the current. The bias also reduces the carrier transit time and hence reduces the frequency of collision with the scattering agents. The CB effect is prominent and sustain for long whether we increase temperature or photon power density to a limit; this is because the low field energy is not sufficient to overcome the Coulomb blockade. Therefore, any combination among three parameters, i.e. temperature, photon power density and the electric field (bias voltage) has to satisfy the requirement of charging energy to overcome the Coulomb blockade potential.

Fig. 9 (a–f) shows how the CB induced carrier tunneling starts and disappears as a function of bias voltage while the detector was kept at two different temperatures of 103 K and 138 K; and at each temperature two laser excitation wavelengths (405 nm and 1064 nm) were used for taking measurements. For clear presentation, the plots in Fig. 9(a and b) have been multiplied from top to bottom by 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2 and 1 respectively; the plots in Fig. 9(d) are multiplied from top to bottom by 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2 and 1; and the plots in Fig. 9(e) are multiplied by the factors: 9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, and 1 respectively from top to bottom.

The effect of bias voltage on the onset of Coulomb blockade energy oscillations (CBO), envelope formation of CBO and PTPA of oscillations has been studied for laser wavelengths 405 nm and 1064 nm at 103 K and 138 K and shown in Fig. 9. These measurements have been performed up to 20 V bias voltage. A general trend of decrease in PTPA amplitude of CBO with increasing bias voltage at both temperatures and excitation wavelengths has been observed. The difference in oscillation amplitude at different temperatures is expected as the circuit receives more current at high temperatures (138 K) due to collection of high density of charge carriers that tunnel through the barrier and this is reflected in the CB oscillation amplitude. The oscillations almost die out at 3 V for 405 nm and 8 V for 1064 nm at 138 K (Fig. 9(c and f)). The decrease in amplitude of oscillations can be understood as: Coulomb force due to interdot coupling gets weakened, thereby overcoming the charging energy at elevated temperature sand high bias voltages (Figs. 8 and 9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD arrays: theory of photocarrier generation and loss under photoexcitation, temperature and bias field
ce:section.ce:para:
Regardless of the fact that it contains several atoms within it, a semiconductor quantum dot (QD) has discrete atomic-like energy levels. This discreteness of energy levels results in narrow optical emission lines at low temperatures. However, when a group of QDs (having different sizes) is excited, the size distribution of QDs results in broadening of discrete emission lines. General working principle of a solid-state QD photodetector is demonstrated in Fig. 10 , and it involves the processes marked as follows [104–110]:
(a)
carrier generation by absorbing incident photons in the left side of barrier layer sp3 bonded region either from the defect states to its conduction band, or
(b)
generation of carriers by absorbing incident photons in the left side of barrier layer sp3 bonded region through inter band transition,
(c)
charging energy induced transition from conduction band of rGO (sp3 region) to available state in graphene QD, and
(d)
further transition from QD to conduction band of the rGO (sp3) of the right side of sp3 bonded region.

The generated carriers may be lost or not be able to reach to the terminal electrode due to the following processes:
(e)
when the energy level of photogenerated carriers in left side barrier layer is considerably higher than the empty state of a quantum dot, the carriers try to occupy these QD states by absorbing charging energy. Once inside the QD, the carriers are entrapped and eventually relax to QD ground state by emission of phonons (LO, LA, TA or combinational modes).
(f)
electron-hole pair recombination in the sp3 zone,
(g)
multiple scattering with phonons, defects, edge boundary of the films in the sp3 zone, and lastly

A photodetector works by generating the carriers (from one or more processes described above in a-d) within the semiconducting layer and driving these carriers to an external circuit till they recombine [66]. While travelling through channel, these carriers may be trapped by defects present in the bandgap or the semiconductor/metal interface. Subsequently de-trapping occurs by absorbing energy of incident photon or thermal vibrations. Thus, under irradiation, confined carriers will have the tendency to surmount the capture potential and reach their bands and do not recombine. This can be attributed to two factors: firstly, the energy of incident photons is much higher than the trapping potential, and secondly, photon flux is high enough to decrease effective carrier recombination rates [110].

In case of graphene like materials, interband and Auger recombination processes at all temperatures lead to an equalization of carrier generation and recombination rates [33,66,110–112]. In case of trap supported recombination, two processes dominate: (a) Shockley-Read-Hall (R
SRH ) [66,107–110], and (b) Auger (R
Auger ) [48,112–116] recombination. If carrier density is high, we have [107]:
(6)
R
SRH
=
α
e
α
h
V
t h
N
t
r
2
n
e
n
h
(
n
e
∝
e
+
α
h
n
h
)
d
≅
α
e
V
t h
N
t
d
r
n
e
(7)
R
Auger
=
C
A u g e r
(
rn
e
)
2
α
N
t
(
rn
e
)
2
d
Here α
e(h) is capture cross-section to entrap electrons (holes), and N t is trap areal density. Under irradiation, electrons transit between conduction band and trap/defect states. The number of electrons in conduction band or trap states may change due to the following processes [33]:

(a) In conduction band: photogeneration, carrier multiplication, recombination and relaxation to trap states may change the number of electrons [33], and (b) In trap/defect states: excitation to conduction band, relaxation from conduction band, and recombination with holes in valence band [33] change the number of electrons.

The electron capture rate (α) of trap states has been given by Yongzee Zhang et al. [33] as:
(8)
α =
1
e χ β
(
τ
t
R
τ
1
)
where
τ
1
is lifetime of trapped electrons and
τ
t
is the transition time. The number of electrons generated per photon absorption caused by electron-hole impact ionization is χ, β is photogeneration rate, and R represents recombination rate of electrons. Both α and R exhibit temperature dependence. However, in graphene, R ∼1 ps [44] and it does not change much owing to high thermal stability. Thus, α, or trapping cross-section rate of electrons by trap/defect states, solely determines the temperature induced photodetection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wavelength dependent sensing response in GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Photoresponse was measured as the variation in current with change in excitation wavelength (λexc ∼ 405, 635, and 1064 nm) at ∼1.4 mW/mm2 laser power and results are shown in Fig. 11 (a). When laser is turned ON, an abrupt increase in current is observed which soon returns to its dark current value as the laser is switched OFF. The change in current for light OFF/ON condition is higher in case of 1064 nm excitation vis-à-vis 635 nm and 405 nm. In case of 1064 nm, the carrier excitation takes place from the defect states in sp3 zone and subsequent passing on to the terminal electrodes as mentioned earlier, whereas 405 nm and 635 nm excitation correspond to band to band transition and 635 nm gives better response because it has more resonance effect in the band to band transition process. The detector response profile is stable, repeatable and negligible baseline drift in case of 1064 nm excitation vis-à-vis 405 nm and 635 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operating temperature dependent GQD sensor response when illuminated with laser wavelength 1064 nm
ce:section.ce:para: Operating temperature dependent photoresponse study was conducted in the temperature range of 273–93 K (Fig. 12 ). During laser exposure, operating temperature of the photodetector is maintained by Linkam T95-PE temperature controller fitted with sensor chamber. The detector performance shows activated energy induced response from 273 K to 183 K as seen in non-CB regime having no Coulomb blockade energy oscillation (CBO); below which Coulomb blockade-as well as carrier tunneling effect sets in and continues till 93 K. CBO becomes stronger as we move towards lower temperature. Interestingly, CBO is visible in the baseline when laser is OFF; and for sudden exposure to laser excitation pulse, response shoots up and maintains steady state oscillation till the laser is OFF, and this is as per the carrier generation and loss theory given earlier.

We have also observed CB oscillations in envelope shape (Fig. 12). The envelope formation initiates at 138 K and its oscillation amplitude increases as the operating temperature is decreased further. The formation of envelope can be attributed to overlapping of electron wave functions in between QDs caused by interdot coupling due to Coulomb force. The average QD size found was in 3–6 nm range where the energy levels in CB and VB are discrete due to confinement effect. When coupling begins, the electron/hole wave functions start interfering and splitting of energy levels takes place; resulting in the formation of subbands. At higher temperatures, Coulomb force is weakened by thermal energy that led to disappearance of envelope waveform formation.

Surprisingly, this envelope looks like– (a) amplitude modulated signal where the message signal is superimposed over carrier frequency in communication engineering [117], (b) magnetic field resulted de-Haas van Alphen effect [118], and (c) the result of superposition of an ensemble of waves with slightly different wavelengths while travelling through a medium [119]. Interestingly, the oscillations of envelope and wave within the envelope are periodic with a frequency of oscillation being 0.61 Hz and 10 Hz, respectively. In this case, there is neither high frequency source nor any magnetic field is applied to the sensing setup. Thus, we can ignore the first two cases and the third seems most plausible [55].

The variation of response- and recovery time with operating temperature of the photodetector is shown in Fig. 13 . Both response- and recovery-time demonstrate weak dependence over temperature. Due to involvement of thermally induced electron-phonon scattering at elevated temperatures, response- and recovery-time are expected to increase with increasing temperature. The observed variation in response- and recovery-time is in agreement with this fact.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoresponsitivity and operating temperature of GQD based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14 plots the variations in photocurrent, photoresponsivity and sensitivity of fabricated device with operating temperature. It can be concluded from the figure that maximum response is obtained for 1064 nm excitation in both the CB (up to 138 K) and non-CB (>138 K) regime. Up to 400 K, the response is found to be stable for all the wavelengths followed by high instability along with decrease in response; and the picture is better for 635 nm but photoresponsivity is very little. Such performance may be explained in terms of the following reasons-

The instability and decrease in response at higher temperatures, i.e. in the non-CB zone may be explained in the light of carrier scattering in the sp3 bonded region. The sp3 bonded rGO zone comprises many defects or trapping states which affect carrier density in conduction band. These defect states trap photogenerated electrons till the temperature is not high enough to overcome the capture probability of trap states. To satisfy charge conservation requirement in conduction channel, multiple hole-circulation takes place after generation of an electron-hole pair. Electron-hole cannot recombine before overcoming the capture probability [48,104,116]. Therefore, high temperature operation leads to a reduction in electron capture probability and free carriers are released to the conduction band. This results in the transition of electrons from defect energy states to the conduction band.

The factors delaying carrier recombination and increasing electron transit time (
τ
t r
)
to reach the terminal electrodes include scattering of electrons with phonons, electrons, defects, grain boundary/edges, etc. [120]. Electron-hole pairs can also recombine non-radiatively resulting in reducing the number of free carriers. But this can be avoided by applying an optimum bias (V bias ) across the photodetector. Bias voltage drifts the carriers to be collected by the terminal electrodes. Additionally, recombination loss probability is again decreased because of the abovementioned scattering agents. At high temperatures, random scattering is enhanced, thereby further reducing recombination probability. The carrier transit time (
τ
t r
) increases with scattering factors by a factor of 10P (p ∼ 1) thereby reducing the number of charge carriers (Q) collected at the terminal electrodes [121]. Thus, terminal current (I
terminal = Q/
τ
t r
) is also reduced, which in turn decreases the sensitivity at high temperatures as seen in Fig. 14.

In a nut shell, it appears that there is a contradiction between two vital sensor parameters, i.e. photoresponsitivity and sensitivity as a function of operating temperature. From sensitivity point of view, the sensor performance is good in the CB regime (up to 183 K); whereas the operating temperature may be extended up to 623 K (mostly in non-CB regime) taking into account the photoresponsitivity factor, though sensitivity decreases in this range. Therefore, if we take the advantage of high temperature operation of the proposed QDs based optical detector then it will be at the cost of sensitivity. The extension of operating temperature to such an extent may be due to 3D quantum confinement.

GQDs embedded in rGO/Alumina composite self-standing film have been investigated to study its potential as a photodetector to work in a wide high temperature range with high sensitivity and photoresponsitivity. Studies were conducted over a wide irradiation wavelength range, i.e. from 405 to 1064 nm. The maximum photoresponse has been observed for 1064 nm laser illumination both in Coulomb blockade (CB) and non-blockade regime. Temperature dependent studies suggest linear and stable response in the CB regime; and operating temperature may even be extended up to 623 K even though it is in non-CB regime. Maximum sensitivity of 37.9% has been observed at 93 K for 1064 nm illumination at a power density of 1.4 mW/mm2. Whereas, highest photoresponsitivity of 0.96 mA/W is observed at 533 K for 1064 nm laser with power density of 1.4 mW/mm2. Response- and recovery-time were found to increase with increasing operating temperature. Power dependent studies show linear relation between power density and photoresponsitivity. A contradictory behavior between temperature dependent photoresponsitivity and sensitivity shows that both the parameters cannot be achieved with optimum efficiency when temperature of operation is concerned. From sensitivity point of view, the sensor performance is good in the CB regime (up to 183 K) whereas from the perspective of photoresponsivity, the operating temperature may be extended up to 623 K (in non-CB regime). Therefore, if we take the advantage of high temperature operation of the proposed optical detector then it will be at the cost of sensitivity. The extension of operating temperature to such an extent may be understood due to 3D quantum confinement. As widely recognized, graphene exhibits good compatibility with silicon and thus can be readily integrated in various applications including photonics and optoelectronics. Such comprehensive studies on optical detection by GQD array in CB and non-CB regimes over a temperature range 93–623 K is the first report to the best of our knowledge.
